text:
Summarize (Orchestrating the Discussion)

Discussing Solutions and Strategies

  • Watch and lsiten as students explain their strategies for answering the questions. Get a sense for students' confidence when solving ratio problems. Select a variety of student work to show different strategies, including unit rates, ratio tables, multiplying/dividing both quantities in a ratio (scaling) to find larger or smaller values as needed, and algebraic expression to generate equivalent ratios. (Portrayal)

Making the Mathematics Explicit

Make sure that students are able to generalize how to recognize unit rate situations as specials ratios.

Suggested Questions

  • How did this problem seem different from and seem the same as previous problems in the unit?
  • How did you determine wha to do with the recipe?

Repeat this questioning with other ingredients that you heard groups discussing during the Explore.

  • How can you explain how to recognize a unit rate?

• Answers will vary. It is a ratio situation like all of the situations in the unit. To scale the ratios, you did not always use whole numbers.
  • Zack needs to use 1 1/2 of each ingredient.
  • It is a comparison of two quantities where one of the two quantities is 1.

Now What Do Students Know?

Ask students to reflect on the discussion and answer the Now What Do You Know? questions.

See answers to the problem for more information about the strategies and embedded mathematics.

Reflecting on Student Learning

  • Use the following questions to assess students understanding at the end of the lesson.
    • What evidence so I have that students understand the Now What Do You Know? question?
      • Where did my students get stuck?
      • What strategies did they use?
      • What breakthroughs did my students have today?
    • How will I use this to plan for tomorrow? For the next time I teach this lesson?
    • Where will I have the opportunity to reinforce these ideas as I continue through this unit? The next unit?
